answer:
Postgres queue. 50k/day ≈ 0.6 jobs/sec — trivial. Postgres queues (with `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) handle thousands/sec.

Why:
- Zero new infra, one system to monitor/backup.
- Transactional enqueue: job commits atomically with your business data — kills a whole class of "row saved but job lost" bugs Redis/SQS can't avoid without outbox patterns.
- Easy inspection/retry via SQL.

Tooling: Rails → Solid Queue or GoodJob. Node → pg-boss or Graphile Worker. Python → Procrastinate. Go → River. Elixir → Oban.

Switch triggers (none apply at 50k/day):
- Sustained >1–2k jobs/sec or millions/day → Redis/SQS.
- Queue churn causing table bloat/vacuum pressure on a hot Postgres → separate DB or Redis.
- Need cross-service fan-out at scale → SQS/SNS.

Caveat: put jobs on the same Postgres instance only if it has headroom; jobs generate write/vacuum load. At your volume, negligible.
